name: Cooney Hills
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in the Platte County area featuring agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Free personal use collecting with no permit required and gentle terrain suitable for f...
description:
Cooney Hills is a public BLM rockhounding area in Platte County, Wyoming, offering excellent opportunities for collectors of all experience levels. The site sits at 5,546 feet elevation with gentle slopes that make it accessible for family outings and beginner rockhounders. The terrain features exposed geological formations typical of the Laramie Range foothills, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and later sedimentary deposits have weathered to expose beautiful agates, jaspers, and chalcedony specimens.

The Cooney Hills area lies within the High Plains transition zone where ancient geological uplift created favorable conditions for silica-based mineral concentrations. Over millennia, weathering and erosion have exposed these colorful stones on slopes and in small draws. The gently sloping terrain makes surface collecting straightforward - specimens can often be spotted without extensive digging or tools.

Collectors typically find specimens ranging from thumbnail size to several inches across. Common colors include banded agates in reds, oranges, and creams, plus jasper specimens in earth tones. The chalcedony here often shows the translucency that makes these stones prized for cabochons and display pieces. Early morning or late afternoon visits provide better visibility for spotting specimens against the hillside backdrop.

This is free personal use collecting on public BLM land with minimal restrictions. The remote location means fewer crowds than fee dig sites, and the mild terrain allows you to cover substantial ground in a few hours. Bring plenty of water, sturdy boots for uneven footing, and a collection bag to haul your finds.
history: The Cooney Hills area sits within the Laramie Range, a Precambrian metamorphic uplift that was rejuvenated during Laramide orogeny in the Late Cretaceous. Silica-rich fluids percolated through sedimentary and metamorphic host rocks, depositing the agates and chalcedony now weathering from hillsides. The region has a long history of informal rockhounding dating back decades, with local collectors familiar with the productive slopes.
tips: Search the gentle south and east-facing slopes where erosion has exposed fresh material - focus on small washes and areas with surface scatter of colorful stones. Bring a 10x loupe to examine specimens before packing them; look for banding patterns in agates and the translucent quality that indicates quality chalcedony. Use a geologist's hammer and chisels only for in-place extraction from exposed seams - most keepers can be collected directly from the surface. Early season (spring) often yields the best finds as winter freeze-thaw cycles dislodge specimens from parent rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,546 feet with gentle to moderate slopes across open sagebrush country. Terrain is rocky with scattered loose stones and uneven footing. Vegetation consists primarily of sagebrush, grasses, and low shrubs. Walking distances from parking areas range from 0.25 to 1.5 miles depending on where you focus. No defined trails; you will walk cross-country across hillsides. Footing is moderate difficulty with loose rock common.
best_season: April - October. The area is accessible year-round, but late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable weather. Summer afternoons can be hot and exposed with little shade. Fall provides excellent conditions with cooler temperatures and low humidity. Winter snowfall can make access difficult at this elevation.
written_directions: From Wheatland (the nearest town with services, approximately 12 miles south), take WY-34 north toward Saratoga. Continue on WY-34 for approximately 8-10 miles. Look for BLM access points marked with standard BLM signage on the east side of the highway near the Cooney Hills area. Parking is available at informal pullouts along the road or at small parking areas accessed by short dirt spurs. GPS coordinates point directly to the central collecting area. The final approach may require high-clearance vehicle or high-clearance SUV depending on road conditions.
road_conditions: Paved highway access via WY-34. Final approach roads are dirt and may be washboard or rutted, typically passable with standard passenger vehicle in dry conditions. High-clearance vehicle recommended after rain.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts and dirt parking spurs available along WY-34. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-8 vehicles depending on location. No facilities at parking areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. Collecting is limited to surface specimens and loose material - do not damage geological formations or extract from solid rock without proper authorization. Pack out all trash and leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation equipment
Collect surface specimens and loose material only
No damage to geological formations
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Wheatland, Wyoming (approximately 12 miles south) has gas, food, lodging, and water. Limited services in Saratoga to the north.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle sloping terrain accessible to most hikers with moderate fitness. Uneven rocky footing and scattered loose stones require sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access. Walking distances up to 1.5 miles cross-country. Most collecting can be done within 0.5 miles of parking.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Cooney Hills, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Cooney Hills is known for beautiful agate specimens, jasper in earth tones, chalcedony, and quartz. Agates here often display banding in reds, oranges, and creams. Chalcedony specimens frequently show the translucency that makes them excellent for cutting into cabochons. Most specimens found are thumbnail to several inches in size.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Cooney Hills?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collecting at Cooney Hills. This is public BLM land in Wyoming where recreational rockhounding is allowed free of charge. You must follow the 25 lb per day limit and personal use only restrictions per FLPMA regulations.

faq.question: How far is Cooney Hills from Wheatland, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Cooney Hills is approximately 12 miles north of Wheatland via WY-34. Wheatland is the nearest town with full services including gas, food, lodging, and water supplies. The drive takes roughly 15-20 minutes from Wheatland.

faq.question: Is Cooney Hills rockhounding site beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Cooney Hills is very beginner-friendly. The gentle terrain requires no special climbing skills, and most quality specimens can be found on the surface without extensive digging. Kids ages 6 and up can participate. The open sagebrush slopes make it easy to spot colorful stones, and no specialized equipment is required beyond a collection bag and comfortable boots.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Cooney Hills?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best rockhounding at Cooney Hills, with peak conditions in May-June and September-October when temperatures are mild. Summer can be hot and exposed. Fall is ideal with cooler temperatures and excellent visibility. Winter snowfall makes access difficult at 5,546 feet elevation.

faq.question: Can you bring dogs rockhounding at Cooney Hills, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on BLM land at Cooney Hills. Bring plenty of water for your dog as there are no water sources at the site, and the sagebrush country offers no shade. Keep your dog under control and be aware of wildlife in the area, particularly snakes and coyotes.

meta_description: Free rockhounding at Cooney Hills, Wyoming. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony on public BLM land. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit, beginner friendly.
